data.title: A Three-Pronged Major Market Rotation Is Just Beginning
data.description:
Ever since early 2023, when ChatGPT went mainstream and Nvidia (NVDA) delivered blowout guidance, the S&P 500 (SPY) has been in a seemingly irreversible upward climb, and the NASDAQ (QQQ) has surged even more as investors have piled into AI-related tech and software stocks due to an expectation that AI would dominate the global economy in the coming years and decades.

Meanwhile, persistently high inflation rates and, with it, persistently high long-term interest rates have hurt real asset companies, especially in the REIT (VNQ) and the broader high-yield investing universe (DIV), as these stocks floundered from the beginning of 2022 through the end of 2025.

However, due to three major factors, there is a significant rotation underway in which, so far this year, software companies (IGV) and increasingly AI leaders at large (XLK) are seeing their stock prices decline, while real asset companies or real asset investments are ascending.

In this article, I will detail what these three reasons are and why I think we are just in the early stages of this market rotation.

AI Is Eating Software Moats

One big reason is that the rise in AI, especially with recent applications of it developed by companies like Anthropic and OpenAI, has caused the market to have serious concerns about the sustainability of many digital-first businesses and, in particular, software companies. The concern is that, with AI becoming an extremely talented software engineer, what used to require immense sums of capital and skilled software engineering labor can now be done in a short period of time by a very small team of humans with the aid of AI coding tools. As a result, the moats around many of these software businesses are deemed to be much narrower than previously thought or, in some cases, even non-existent.

While some are claiming that many software companies will even possibly go extinct, and you can see this in the rapid deterioration of stock prices for many of these companies, I personally think this is unlikely, especially for the more well-established ones that have high switching costs. However, their profit margins are likely to shrink given the fact that very similar software can be created at a low cost, which will likely lead to an abundance of competition and will force them to reduce their profit margins and likely reduce demand for their products. While the software companies with deeply embedded partnerships with companies will likely retain many of those, they will also probably have to make some concessions on the price front and potentially even the volume front to keep that business in place.

Therefore, no matter how you slice and dice it, I think that it is highly likely that a large number of software companies will experience permanent impairment to their valuation relative to where it was before the rise of high-quality AI coding tools. What this means is that investors are no longer seeing AI companies and software companies as the safe havens that they once were, as they, in many cases, are becoming the victims of their own success, with the rapid rise of AI leveling the playing field and allowing new entrants into industries that they previously dominated.

As a result, investors are rotating capital out of these businesses that they previously viewed as safe, growth-at-a-reasonable-cost, growth-at-a-reasonable-price investments, and instead are putting that capital into AI-resistant and AI-proof industries. Perhaps the most AI-proof and AI-resistant of all are real asset industries because, whether it be land, energy, or natural resources and commodities, these are not things that can be created out of thin air by AI. Instead, they will become the new primary constraints of economic productivity since intelligence and manpower will likely not be in the not-too-distant future due to the wide proliferation of low-cost to even free AI tools.

De-Dollarization Is Accelerating Faster Than Most Investors Realize

The second big reason why this market shift is taking place is because of the ongoing and arguably even accelerating de-dollarization trend. This was already seen several years ago with the skyrocketing price of gold (GLD) that then picked up steam with silver (SLV), platinum (PPLT), and palladium (PALL) also soaring last year, and is now spreading across the broader real asset space.

Global de-dollarization is being fueled in large part by persistent budget deficits in the United States, with no end in sight. Additionally, the harsh sanctions placed on Russia following its invasion of Ukraine have caused many other would-be adversaries of the United States, and in particular China, to reconsider how much exposure they have to dollars in their reserves. Finally, the remaking of the global order in the wake of President Trump's tariff policy and diplomatic negotiation styles is also causing global institutions to consider diversifying away from the dollar.

These forces are all combining to prompt global institutions, which until recently were content to hoard dollars in the form of Treasuries (TLT) and other dollar-denominated paper assets, to de-dollarize and de-risk away from the dollar in the United States. Instead, they are putting those dollars into real assets that can withstand inflation and a potential dollar crisis in the future. Not only that, but these assets, in some cases, are not as easy to seize and sanction. Obviously, gold and silver and other metals and commodities fit into that category, but also, increasingly, energy commodities and stocks (XLE) are getting a boost, as witnessed by the soaring price of many energy companies (XOM)(CVX).

Infrastructure (UTF), utilities (XLU), and real estate have also been getting a boost from the market recently, and I expect that trend to continue moving forward as de-dollarization continues.

Meanwhile, AI and software companies are not real assets and, therefore, are not inherently likely to soar as inflation picks up, especially when considering the aforementioned point that AI will likely weaken their moats and, as a result, eat into their profit margins. This is why megacap AI and software powerhouses like Microsoft (MSFT) and Palantir (PLTR) have been declining sharply recently:

$700 Billion In AI CapEx: Who Really Wins?

Finally, this rotation is also being driven by the combination of lofty valuations in the mega-cap technology and AI sectors relative to pretty cheap valuations in many real asset sectors. Moreover, especially in the mega-cap tech space, the enormous amounts of money, to the tune of about $600 to $700 billion expected this year alone as mega-cap tech stocks are sinking into AI-related CapEx, are giving many in the market pause about the returns they can expect on new dollars being deployed at current valuations. That is because the vast amount of money that's being sunk into AI-related infrastructure, including data centers and energy required to power those data centers, has an uncertain return profile, and the relatively low amount of revenue currently being generated from AI products on a comparative basis means that there is going to have to be an enormous ramp-up in AI-related revenue for these types of numbers to make any sort of sense. Additionally, there is significant innovation risk because, while AI is advancing rapidly in some areas, it still has a long way to go, especially in the area of agentic AI and robotics, before it can truly manifest the sort of economic impact that is currently being priced into many AI-related stocks.

Not only that, but many real asset businesses, especially infrastructure and energy companies, are positioned to profit significantly from AI CapEx spending, whether it be midstream (AMLP) giants like Enbridge (ENB), TC Energy (TRP), Kinder Morgan (KMI), The Williams Companies (WMB), and others, or energy power producers like Constellation Energy (CEG) and Vistra Corp. (VST), or even more diversified AI infrastructure plays like Brookfield Infrastructure Partners (BIP)(BIPC), Brookfield Renewable Partners (BEP)(BEPC), and other data center and infrastructure plays. Therefore, it would seem to be a much safer bet to put money into the picks and shovels plays, especially given that it can be done at reasonable valuations and, in some cases, very attractive dividend yields at the moment, rather than betting on high-priced AI innovators and CapEx investors with a much more uncertain return profile.

Investor Takeaway

Simply buying the S&P 500 and Nasdaq has been a tremendously profitable investing strategy for the past several years. However, in my view, this run has likely come to an end as the market is now rotating capital out of the AI and tech plays that have led those industries higher and instead is pouring into real asset businesses.

With AI posing as a big threat to tech company moats, de-dollarization pushing global institutions out of treasuries and into real assets, massive AI capex putting tech powerhouses increasingly at risk while providing a strong growth catalyst for some real asset businesses, and the large valuation gap between tech and real asset businesses beginning to close, the ongoing market rotation appears likely to continue for the foreseeable future.

For investors who want to take advantage of this ongoing market rotation, de-risk their portfolios in the face of AI disruption, and lock in attractive and growing passive income, identifying and buying undervalued, high-quality, and high-yielding real asset investments looks like a great buying opportunity right now. At High Yield Investor, we have leaned heavily into this trend with a 7%+ yielding portfolio that, over the past five years, has delivered significant total return outperformance with below-market beta. If this ongoing rotation continues as we expect, we believe owners of these types of real asset stocks will likely enjoy further outperformance in the coming years.

data.title: Nebius: The Devil Is In The Details
data.description:
Nebius Group N.V. (NBIS) has delivered a strong Q4 earnings report that continue to support momentum through 2026, given resilient AI compute demand and pricing dynamics. Specifically, the company’s full year annual recurring revenue (“ARR”) reached $1.2 billion exiting 2025, outpacing the high-end of its earlier guidance. This continues to reinforce support for Nebius’ maintained ARR guidance for 2026 at $7 billion to $9 billion, which implies another three-fold jump in 2027 revenue. The results underscore extended visibility into Nebius’ growth trajectory and market share gains, backed by a combination of improving cloud contract duration, volume and pricing dynamics. Nebius’ execution is further corroborated by its narrowing operating and adjusted EBITDA losses.

However, a deeper dive into Nebius’ latest fundamental composition continues to cast uncertainties to its earnings inflection and free cash flow (“FCF”) accretion prospects. As discussed in my previous coverage on Nebius, underlying fundamentals remain prone to volatile component cost pressures, while implications from the adverse audit opinion on its internal control for financial reporting (“ICFR”) environment remain a risk overhang. This is especially relevant to Nebius, given the durability of the AI investment thesis has become increasingly hinged on ROI visibility and quality to compensate for rising capital intensity. Although Nebius’ execution consistency in expanding ARR is backed by strong demand, its pathway to a scalable earnings inflection and capital self-sufficiency remains uncertain. This is further pressured by its accelerating capex trajectory ahead, which is expected to jump almost five-fold to as much as $20 billion this year.

At 7.3x NTM sales and 18.4x NTM EBITDA, Nebius is now trading near multiples exhibited across its hyperscaler counterparts like Microsoft (MSFT), which are inherently better de-risked given proven profitability and capital self-sufficiency at scale. Although Nebius’ multi-fold growth outlook clearly outpaces that of Big Tech peers trading at comparable multiples, the stock’s current valuation premium continues to embed expectations for a sustained profitability inflection that remains unproven. As a result, limited visibility into its earnings and FCF accretion trajectory is likely to remain a persistent multiple compression risk, challenging the stock’s upside potential from current levels.

ROI Inflection Remains Uncertain

Nebius’ headline earnings metrics corroborate strong execution in 2025. The company’s revenues accelerated 547% y/y to $227.7 million in Q4, contributing significantly to the 355% y/y increase in full year 2025 sales. The continuity of this momentum was corroborated by $1.2 billion in ARR exiting 2025, with 2026 revenue guided at almost 3x that metric between $3 billion and $3.4 billion. The company also reported narrowing adjusted EBITDA loss, as full year 2025 operating margins show improvement.

Looking ahead, management expects Nebius to exit 2026 with ARR between $7 billion and $9 billion. If the approximately three-fold ARR-to-revenue conversion implied by 2026 guidance holds into 2027, and capacity and demand continues to scale in lock-step, then this could translate to as much as $27 billion in revenue next year. This would imply a 7x revenue step-up from 2026, underscoring potential scale benefits ahead.

However, a deeper dive into Nebius’ cost functions continue to shed uncertainties on its ability to scale operations, limiting visibility into its earnings inflection outlook and AI returns prospects.

Specifically, the company’s operating losses grew 80% sequentially in Q4, far outpacing the 56% q/q revenue growth observed over the same period. This was primarily driven by higher sales and depreciation costs, which jumped 79% and 83% q/q, respectively. Although Nebius continues to sell out before capacity even comes online, underscoring a robust demand environment, supported by expanding contract durations that underscore growth visibility, its accelerating SG&A spend highlights rising customer acquisition costs. The anticipated extension to its GPU asset depreciation schedule from four years to five years starting Q1 is also unlikely to fully reverse the sizable jump in recent depreciation and amortization expense, especially as capital investments intensify.

Specifically, management guides $16 billion to $20 billion in capex this year, which represents a more than four-fold jump from $4 billion deployed in 2025. Although headline guidance suggests up to 536% y/y revenue growth in 2026 on 4x capex expansion, which implies improving capital efficiency, it isn’t conclusive of sustained ROI expansion.

Nebius’ AI returns outlook remains dictated by its ability to scale capacity expansion and utilization, which also hinges on the timing of contract fulfilment and subsequent revenue recognition. This accordingly exposes it to ongoing fluctuations in external cost factors across the broader AI value chain, spanning both infrastructure and power cost implications, in particular.

As discussed in the previous coverage, power constraints remain an intensifying bottleneck on AI developments. Although Nebius has been proactive in power procurement, raising its contracted capacity target from 2GW previously to 3GW by year-end, its rapid expansion roadmap remains prone to energy cost inflation risks driven by broader industry supply constraints.

The contracted power doesn’t fully insulate Nebius’ unit economics from, given the timing lag in converting to available data center capacity. Specifically, only about 800MW to 1GW of the contracted power is expected to be operational and revenue-generating by year-end. This effectively keeps a meaningful portion of Nebius’ procured energy exposed to risks of pricing reset as deployment progresses, amplified by rising costs of converting the contracted supply into operational capacity amid strained utilities and data center supply chains. The persistent uncertainties to scaling its core AI cloud unit economics also risks being further exacerbated by rising sales costs, as discussed in the earlier section.

Management’ expectation for the majority of expanded capacity this year to come online in 2H26 also implies a meaningful lag between capex deployment and revenue recognition. The timing gap could risk furthering uncertainty to Nebius’ near-term AI returns trajectory – similar to what’s been recently observed at Microsoft. Specifically, Microsoft’s been a key beneficiary of secular AI opportunities, especially in the core infrastructure layer. However, this accelerating demand environment continues to be bottlenecked by power and compute supply availability. In Microsoft’s case, capex has been repeatedly on the rise in recent quarters to bring additional capacity online. Yet a commensurate uplift to near-term growth remains absent, harbingering AI ROI compression risks. And for Nebius, similar exposure could be a larger consequential downside risk, given its persistent losses and negative FCF leave less room for absorbing risks of related cost overruns as new capacity buildout scales.

Operating Cash Flow Visibility Could Be Temporary

Nebius’ operating cash flows have turned positive in Q4, driven primarily by upfront cash payments received on long-term customer commitments. And management expects the dynamic to persist throughout the remainder of the year:

We ended the year with $3.7 billion in cash and cash equivalents and generated $834 million in operating cash flow in Q4, which was primarily comprised of upfront payments from our long-term agreements. These early cash flows will continue over the course of the year as we execute against these commitments, providing us with significant visibility into future cash flow.

Source: Nebius Q4 2025 Earnings Call Transcript.

This is corroborated by management’s expectation for consistent execution on several large contracts with hyperscalers, including contracted capacity deployment for Meta Platforms (META) and Microsoft which will continue through 2026 as planned.

The pulled forward cash receipts are constructive for Nebius’ rising capital intensity, with capex expected to rise by more than 4x this year to as much as $20 billion, which management intends to fund up to 60% with internal resources. The improved near-term cash flows could also improve Nebius’ access to external financing, which is relevant given the company’s intentions to tap into corporate debt and asset-backed financing to fund its accelerating growth investments.

However, the near-term operating cash flow inflection is driven primarily by working capital changes stemming from higher deferred revenue and customer prepayments rather than earnings accretion at scale. This suggests continued uncertainties to the sustainability of Nebius’ operating cash flow generation. The durability of Nebius’ timing-driven cash flow profile is further undermined by its widening adjusted net loss, as operating leverage remains pressured by increasing sales costs while infrastructure and product development spend stays elevated amid rapid AI advancement cycles.

Taken together, the front-loaded operating cash inflow has yet to validate scalability in Nebius’ underlying unit economics. Without scale, the current pace of operating cash generation could lack durability – revenue might continue to accelerate as contracted performance obligations are fulfilled, but the ensuing cash impact remains at risk of stalling as customer payments have been largely pulled forward. While management’s expectation for sustained positive operating cash flow in 2026 could help de-risk near-term funding needs amid the impending step-up in both operating spend and capex, it’s yet to prove that Nebius’ AI investment returns are structurally inflecting on a cash basis. This disconnect effectively undermines the growth and earnings expectations currently embedded in Nebius’ valuation premium, representing a looming risk overhang that remains underappreciated as the stock trades near multiples of its better de-risked hyperscaler peers.

Final Thoughts

Admittedly, Nebius’ Q4 results reinforce visibility into its forward growth trajectory. Its better-than-expected full year ARR of $1.2 billion exiting 2025 effectively lends credibility to management’s target of $7 billion to $9 billion ARR by the end of the current year. This essentially extends Nebius’ prospective runway for multi-fold revenue growth and market share gains despite intensifying industry competition, reinforced by longer contract duration and resilient pricing dynamics on both current and next-generation GPUs.

However, the AI trade debate is rapidly shifting from “growth at all costs” to “scalable returns,” as investors increasingly demand ROI visibility amid an intensifying capex cycle. With Nebius’ capex set to increase by almost five-fold to $16 billion to $20 billion this year, the stock’s premium increasingly hinges on its ability to convert ARR momentum into sustained ROI expansion and FCF accretion that can tangibly fund continued growth investments. This is particularly relevant, given Nebius’ ability to scale unit economics remains unproven despite visibility into a protracted multi-fold revenue growth trajectory. This is evidenced by Q4’s reacceleration in operating expenses, even as revenue grew by 547%.

And while the positive inflection in operating cash flows is favorable to near-term funding optics, Nebius has yet to establish a sustainable cash generation profile rooted in structural earnings accretion. Coupled with limited visibility into Nebius’ ICFR remediation until its 2025 20-F is filed, the adverse audit opinion represents an incremental risk premium that remains underappreciated. Taken together, the durability of Nebius’ current valuation remains prone to trade-offs, especially in a market that’s become less forgiving of companies with uncertain AI return profiles.

data.title: Wave Life Sciences: WVE-006 Now In Focus, Navigating Opportunity And Risk
data.description:
Thesis: A closer look at WVE-006

Wave Life Sciences’ (WVE) investor confidence has been pretty tightly linked to their pipeline optionality, particularly the early clinical promise we saw in WVE-007 for obesity. Towards the end of last year, we saw strong Phase 1 INLIGHT data, with robust and durable Activin E reductions. There was also potential for fat loss and metabolic improvements. It made for quite a good viewing and boosted investor enthusiasm to say the least. As you know, it drove the stock from around $7.50 all the way up to about $21, which I felt was a reflection of outsized expectations. The potential is there, no doubt; however, we are still quite early in the overall picture, and since then, the share price has normalised to the $12 range. The stock also seems to be on a downward slope with investors coming to terms with both the inherent clinical and regulatory risk in early-stage biotech and the market’s reassessment of near-term catalysts. We have to keep in mind that Wave has a pretty diverse pipeline and that, despite WVE-007 showing good promise, negative sentiment among other candidates can cut the gains we saw from WVE-007.

So whilst WVE-007 is showing meaningful promise as a non-GLP-1 approach to obesity, the more immediate focus, I think, shifts to WVE-006. It's a GalNAc-conjugated RNA editing candidate for alpha-1 antitrypsin deficiency. The recent return of full rights from GSK, I feel, should be viewed as rather concerning. Both in terms of opportunity and risk. Wave now has full control over regulatory strategy, including a potential accelerated approval pathway, but it also assumes all operational, manufacturing, and commercialisation responsibilities, which isn't going to be easy, as I'll explain. So, how the company navigates this transition and the upcoming RestorAATion-2 multidose data release will most likely be a very important near-term indicator of investor confidence. I do feel, however, that there is more upside to Wave stock in the longer term, but with the current trend, I think that share price may continue to decline, which gives me cause to put a hold rating.

3Q25 recap

Last earnings Wave reported a net loss of about $53.9 million for 3Q25, a figure which was a slight improvement from the $61.8 million loss we saw a year ago. It showed slightly better operating leverage despite a continued heavy investment in R&D. The company managed to generate $7.6 million in revenue, with research and development expenses increasing to $45.9 million. Elsewhere, general and administrative expenses jumped to $18.1 million with the company advancing multiple clinical programs. Cash and cash equivalents totaled $196.2 million as of the last quarter. In terms of operating spend, they reported a quarterly operating expense of about $64.0 million, which would imply a quarterly cash burn for the most part in line with its net loss of about $54 million. However, since last quarter, we’ve seen the company add about $72.1 million from its ATM facility and committed GSK milestones. On top of this, back in December, they raised roughly $350 million in gross proceeds from an upsized public offering. So now on a pro forma basis, we’re looking at a total available cash figure of roughly $618 million. Now, if we take the recent quarterly operating spend as a proxy, they have a capital base that supports an estimated runway of around 10 quarters, or about 2.5 years. It pushes Wave’s operational funding into 2028, which is going to be pretty important given their multiple late-stage clinical programs.

The current pipeline

As for the current pipeline, Wave’s portfolio seems increasingly diversified, with a multi-modality approach across RNAi, RNA editing, exon-skipping, and allele-selective oligonucleotide mechanisms. Now, several of their programs are in clinical development addressing both common and rare diseases. Starting with WVE-007, it’s an investigational GalNAc-siRNA targeting INHBE for obesity. So far, it has demonstrated pretty robust, dose-dependent reductions in Activin E in their ongoing INLIGHT trial. There have also been sustained effects supporting infrequent dosing with multiple upcoming data readouts through mid-2026, which we will get into.

Elsewhere, they have an alpha-1 antitrypsin deficiency (AATD), WVE-006. This is a GalNAc-conjugated A-to-I RNA editing oligonucleotide (AIMer) designed to correct the pathogenic Pi*ZZ mutation we see in AATD. So far, in the RestorAATion-2 study, it has managed to achieve key treatment goals, including an impressive restoration of physiologically relevant AAT protein levels. It also managed to reduce the mutant Z-AAT, with additional cohort data also expected in early to mid-2026. Staying on the topic of AIMer, WVE-008 is being developed for PNPLA3 I148M-associated liver disease, and has been advanced as the next clinical candidate with a Clinical Trial Application planned sometime in 2026.

As for Duchenne muscular dystrophy (DMD), WVE-N531 is an exon-skipping oligonucleotide, specifically for boys amenable to exon 53 skipping. It’s shown statistically significant and clinically meaningful improvements in muscle function in Phase 2 and is now on track for a New Drug Application filing in 2026. Finally, we have to mention WVE-003 for Huntington’s disease (HD), which is a first-in-class allele-selective oligonucleotide that is tipped to selectively lower mutant huntingtin (mHTT) whilst also, and very importantly, managing to preserve the wild-type HTT. The SELECT-HD data demonstrated some allele-selective target engagement and decent correlations with structural neurodegeneration. The company is heading towards a potentially registrational Phase 2/3 study following a much-anticipated IND submission by investors. So with these programs, you can see why the last equity raise was so important.

Global rights to WVE-006

As you know, a couple of weeks ago, Wave managed to regain full global rights to its AATD program WVE-006 from GlaxoSmithKline (GSK). At the time, it was a pretty strategic shift in how the asset itself would be advanced and potentially commercialised. You see, it was originally partnered with GSK to leverage its development and commercialisation infrastructure, but now the program has returned to Wave after both companies concluded that Wave is best positioned to efficiently drive late-stage development. This is the narrative we were told based on the strength of emerging clinical data. As you know, WVE-006 is a GalNAc-conjugated RNA editing oligonucleotide seen to correct the pathogenic Pi*ZZ mutation that causes AATD. So, in enabling restoration of the functional AAT protein and also reducing toxic mutant Z-AAT, the candidate can address two issues here, both the lung and liver manifestations of the disease.

Just a little bit of deeper insight on this, since I think it is quite important in relation to the candidates' differentiation in a potential future market. WVE-006 enables edits in the mutant SERPINA1 mRNA, called Pi*ZZ, toward the wild-type sequence. WVE-006 actually increases the production of properly folded, functional AAT protein that can then be secreted into the bloodstream, thereby restoring protease inhibition in the lung. It also protects against neutrophil elastase-driven tissue damage. Now, at the same time, in reducing levels of misfolded Z-AAT protein in hepatocytes, the therapy can decrease intracellular aggregation and endoplasmic reticulum stress in the liver also. So that side of things directly targets the driver of liver inflammation, fibrosis, and cirrhosis. Patients are then getting a dual effect, a boosted circulating functional AAT and also lowered toxic intracellular Z-AAT. This allows for a single therapy to mechanistically treat both the pulmonary and hepatic manifestations of AATD.

This is a limitation of a lot of current therapies, and there was a lot of investor confidence built into this fact earlier in the candidate's development. We saw encouraging data already showing physiologically meaningful AAT restoration and also mutant protein reduction. Wave now has plans to pursue an accelerated approval pathway and is set to engage directly with the FDA on a potential filing strategy, which is good to hear. We can also expect regulatory feedback mid-year. Operationally speaking, this transition now gives Wave full control over development timelines, trial design, and regulatory strategy. Now, whether this is a good or a bad thing, we are yet to find out, but it should allow Wave to retain all future economic upside rather than sharing it with a partner. I think this moves WVE-006 up a notch in the pipeline and should now be seen as more of a potential near-term value driver for the company. There’s upcoming multidose data from the RestorAATion-2 trial sometime in 1Q26 that could support registrational planning. If we see positive signals, this should set up Wave as a standalone developer of the first RNA editing therapy for AATD that actually targets the root genetic cause of the disease.

The risk in WVE-006

Now, I mentioned that this could be viewed through a positive or a negative lens earlier. We should also keep in mind that this can easily be viewed much more sceptically. It seems intuitive that Wave regaining rights to WVE-006 from GlaxoSmithKline is more likely a negative signalhere. The core assumption is that we don't typically see large pharma partners walk away from assets they believe have strong commercial or clinical potential. And no matter how positively both companies framed the press release, it seems somewhat strange, especially after the meaningful data that had been generated.

From this perspective, I think GSK’s decision to relinquish rights would suggest it may have reassessed the risk/reward profile internally. As you know, big pharma companies do this often and with far more information and expertise than we can. So they must have concluded the program is not attractive enough to justify late-stage development and commercialisation. Even if the clinical data we saw were encouraging, the argument here is that Wave now faces a much harder road. They have to take on regulatory strategy, manufacturing scale-up, payer engagement, and then global commercialisation, all of which have to be done alone and also for the first time since they don’t have any approved products on the market yet.

I would also make the argument here that complex oligonucleotide therapies are far from easy to commercialise. You see, the manufacturing and CMC are unusually complex. Oligos need highly specialised synthesis, purification, and quality control, since very small changes in chemistry/stereochemistry can actually affect their activity and safety. So for Wave, taking on this task alone and for the first time, scaling from clinical to commercial volumes won't be easy. I often see regulators scrutinise batch consistency very closely in this sense. So in that sense, I do expect the cost of goods to be higher and tech transfer riskier for Wave moving forward.

The other area I see some risk is around a potential dosing paradigm. It could be the case that it’s unfamiliar to physicians and payers. I often see oligo drugs require chronic injections, whether subcutaneous or intrathecal, and they have unique tissue targeting, such as GalNAc to the liver. They have to rely on biomarker-driven efficacy rather than immediately obvious clinical endpoints. That could complicate labelling and reimbursement a bit. This brings me to the point of patient identification. As you know, it is far is harder, especially in rare genetic diseases, to identify patients. You often need genotyping and biomarker confirmation, and in some cases, longitudinal monitoring of protein correction rather than symptom relief. That's a bit of a drag on rapid uptake. Lastly, with GSK out of the picture, I think the commercial infrastructure is going to be far more expensive relative to patient numbers for Wave. With it targeting such a small population, they’re going to need all the help they can get in the form of speciality sales, medical affairs, nurse support, and possibly market access teams. And keep in mind this is going to be spread over far fewer patients. A large pharma like GSK could have absorbed all that, but a small biotech like Wave is going to struggle to do it efficiently, in my opinion.

Valuation

Wave now trades at a market cap figure of about $2.3 billion, with an enterprise value of about $2.15 billion. It’s a reflection of a valuation that's driven almost entirely by pipeline optionality. Now, given their very minimal commercial revenue and also ongoing losses, which we can expect to continue, traditional earnings-based metrics such as price to earnings are not yet meaningful. So it makes sales and asset-based multiples more appropriate metrics. On this basis, Wave would screen as rather expensive. They hold a trailing EV to sales of about 19.7x and forward EV to sales of 53x, which are several hundred percent above healthcare sector medians. It goes to show us just how much future success is already priced into the stock. Elsewhere, the company trades at a price to book ratio of 16x, again far above sector norms, which implies investors are valuing Wave’s intellectual property and clinical pipeline well above the balance-sheet value of its assets.

Overall, I think Wave’s valuation reflects a pretty high-risk, albeit high-reward profile typical of a late clinical-stage biotech company. Those who choose to buy in at current levels will be putting their confidence in the probability of regulatory success and eventual commercialisation. As for volume, Wave currently trades with an average daily trading volume in the low millions. The recent 20‑session averages have seen numbers around 2.2 to 4.2 million shares, so there is reasonably solid liquidity here for a clinical‑stage biotech name.

Looking ahead

Next, Wave seems poised for several near-term catalysts. WVE-006 data from the multidose cohort of the RestorAATion-2 trial is expected sometime in 1Q26, which should give some light on discussions with the FDA on a potential accelerated approval pathway. Elsewhere, WVE-007 follow-up data from INLIGHT cohorts, including those three and six-month results across multiple dose levels, should come through in mid-2026. Investors should also keep an eye on the next earnings release around March 2nd.

data.title: Birchcliff Energy: Time For The Leverage To Go
data.description:
Birchcliff Energy (BIREF) has long engaged in a debt strategy that has not benefitted shareholders. For any leverage strategy to be used, shareholders should see the results in both dividends and stock share price appreciation. But that is not the case here for as long as I can remember.

The pattern going on here has been happening with variations for at least a decade now. Management is predicting more debt repayment in the fourth quarter. That happens to be a good thing. Now the debt will be down more than 20%, very roughly, from its peak.

Stock Price

But shareholders have been waiting a very long time for the stock price to break out of its range. Production has grown considerably over the last few years. This was often accompanied by an acquisition that I covered in previous articles on this company.

Meanwhile, the stock appears to be stuck in a broad trading range. I covered in the past how Seymour Schulich (who is known more for Franco-Nevada and Euro-Nevada success) often publicly traded this stock between $3 and $6 a share. That is likewise in past articles.

But as shown above, the stock has made little progress from those days. To me, this means that debt is not working in the favor of investors. As such, it probably needs to be paid down to a very low level or just eliminated completely.

The forward earnings shown above are lackluster (on a per-share basis), to say the least, yet management, in the latest presentation, is planning still more growth.

Peyto Comparison

Compared to Peyto, Peyto has been a far better stock performer lately.

Peyto is a company that went public more than a decade ago and quickly became a market darling. But the stock price hit reality during the big natural gas price decline that ended with Covid. Since this stock has been valued a whole lot more, like any ongoing business. But profitable growth of the business is clear from the stock price.

Also notice that the price-earnings ratio here is far lower than it is for Birchcliff. That implies a relatively whole lot more appreciation potential in the future if that Birchcliff price-earnings ratio has any merit.

Back in fiscal year 2023, Peyto uncharacteristically made a large acquisition from Repsol (REPYY) by using both stock and debt. This increased the debt level to a debt ratio higher than normal for this conservative company.

But the company has been repaying the debt and increasing the dividend as well. More importantly, the company has been reporting steadily lower costs to shareholders as well as better profitability at each pricing point, both quarterly and through the monthly president's letter.

When a company uses debt, even conservatively, one should expect results or should expect management to eliminate the debt. Peyto is clearly getting results from leveraging up. Birchcliff should get rid of the debt until there are long-term results to be had.

Birchcliff

Pouce Coupe and, to a certain extent, Gordondale have benefitted from technological advances, with Gordondale being first for this company by a few years. Much of the company's leases were long known for dry gas production. But technology advances have increased the ability to produce rich gas from this same area for several years now.

The company gives a fair amount of expense guidance (which is good). But there is not a goal for EBITDA or even income (subject, of course, to commodity prices). As a result, income does not make the increasing progress one would expect for a company that regularly grows production. Hence the stock price pattern shown earlier.

Now there are some benefits to the land the company has in that technology advances have made liquids-rich intervals available for production. These intervals are frequently extremely profitable as rich gas production.

This company possibly may show some increasing profitability just because of that. That would help at least temporarily with the future outlook.

But what needs to happen here is consistent year-over-year expense control to the extent that this company shows profitability progress throughout the business cycle. There are a lot of companies that measure this and report back to shareholders. Probably the most significant company doing this that I follow is Exxon Mobil (XOM), which reports this progress quarterly.

Any company that skips an issue as important as profitability at various pricing points is likely to make little long-term progress, as is the case here.

Summary

The common stock of this company has been fluctuating (very roughly) between $3 and $6 per share for as long as I can remember (at least a decade). I previously noted how Seymour Schulich would purchase stock at $3 and then sell it at $6. For a trader, this has been as reliable as a clock (considering its upstream).

But for a long-term buy-and-hold decision, there is really no future outside that trading range unless something changes.

The last article noted that there was a long-term debt issue because that ratio does get on the high side of acceptable. Since then, management has been repaying debt (which is good). But this management gets debt down to a “conservative” level and then figures that the job is done. No mention is made of how this influences profitability because it probably does nothing for profitability.

Since we are again getting near the top of the trading range that in the past has been successful, this stock becomes a sell. It will stay that way until this management communicates to shareholders some solid profitability-increasing goals and then demonstrates conclusively that those goals were met.

That could be an uphill climb (to say the least) because management appears to be very satisfied with the way that things are. However, for me, I prefer Peyto because Peyto made leverage work in the favor of shareholders. In the case of Birchcliff, even a little leverage does not seem to do any good. So maybe it is time to begin with a debt-free balance sheet while showing good, solid results with no debt. We can then work our way up from that.

Risks

The risk here is that the past becomes different from the future to the point that in the future management makes and achieves profitability goals. Then the stock price would break out of that long-term trading range that has made so many traders happy.

Future commodity prices are both volatile and low visibility. A severe and sustained commodity price downturn could materially affect the future of the company.

Since this company is using leverage and there does not seem to be much in the way of benefits from that leverage, an unexpected downturn could have some really bad consequences for a company like this. It is what raises the risk of leverage when profitability is not increasing.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Cullen/Frost Bankers Is Great, But Shares Aren't Cheap Enough For An Upgrade
data.description:
Back in November of 2025, I decided to revisit Cullen/Frost Bankers (CFR). Conceptually, I think it is a great business. Management has achieved strong operational and financial performance as of late. Asset quality is robust, and an overall expansion of key aspects of the balance sheet has been occurring. But as a value investor, I am sensitive to how much I pay for the value that I perceive. And at that time, the stock was just a bit too expensive for my liking. This led me, ultimately, to reaffirm the company as a ‘hold’ candidate. Since then, the market has disagreed with my view that shares should perform along the lines of the broader market for the foreseeable future. In fact, the stock has jumped 18.7% since then. This is at a time when the S&P 500 is up 1.7%.

Given this return disparity that has occurred over an undeniably short window of time, I figured it would be a good idea to revisit the company. Honestly, I still feel very much the same as I did back then. Is it possible that shares could continue to outperform the market moving forward? Absolutely. However, at the current price that shares are going for, I cannot bring myself to pull the trigger on it. The stock looks just a bit pricey for my liking, even factoring in the amazing quality that exists here. So in light of that, I believe that maintaining it as a ‘hold’ candidate is the right choice.

The price isn’t right

For the final quarter of the 2025 fiscal year, Cullen/Frost Bankers reported deposits of $42.92 billion. This is a significant amount of money. But in the banking industry, it still makes this more of a mid-sized bank than it does a large one. After all, the market capitalization of the company right now is just $9.18 billion. What I do know is that the overall deposits of the institution did come in above the $42.52 billion the company had during the third quarter. And it also represents a slight increase over the $42.72 billion that management reported for the final quarter of 2024. This is not significant growth, but it's not awful either. One thing I do know is that there aren't any fluctuations here associated with brokered deposits. Management said as much. Instead, the deposits appear to be all organic.

As the value of deposits increased, the value of certain assets should grow also. At the end of 2024, the company had loans of $20.48 billion. By the end of 2025, that number had risen to $21.61 billion. Just like most banks of its size, Cullen/Frost Bankers is quite diverse. For instance, its greatest loan exposure would be the $6.31 billion that's tied up in commercial and industrial loans. However, it also has sizable exposure when it comes to other types of assets. This includes the $3.99 billion of owner-occupied commercial real estate, the $3.77 billion of non-owner-occupied commercial real estate, and the $2.55 billion dedicated to construction and land loans. The firm has a lot of exposure when it comes to certain types of consumer real estate, such as home equity lines of credit, home equity loans, home improvement loans, and one- to four-family loans. Add on top of this $1.09 billion of energy-related loans, with most of that tied up in the production side of things, and this is definitely a solid and diversified player in the banking sector.

Naturally, other aspects of the balance sheet are worthy of our attention also. While the company does focus primarily on loans, it does have a massive amount of capital tied up in securities. In fact, the business ended 2025 with $19.44 billion worth. This represented a big jump over the $18.61 billion reported the previous year. Cash and cash equivalents were quite large as well, amounting to $8.87 billion. This is actually down from the $10.23 billion the company reported the previous year. And over that same window of time, debt increased from $4.59 billion to $4.77 billion.

This paints a picture of a company that is moving around assets in order to optimize financial performance. Although the rise in debt is never great to see, the overall debt that the company has is modest. And with the company focusing more of its capital on securities and, to a lesser extent, loans, it's clear that management is willing to take on a bit of additional risk if it means locking in more attractive yields.

Between these changes and the composition of the balance sheet, which have pushed the net interest margin for the institution up from 3.53% in the final quarter of 2024 to 3.66% in the final quarter of 2025, and the overall growth of the balance sheet, net interest income jumped from $397.4 million to $437.5 million. For the year as a whole, the increase to $1.69 billion from the $1.54 billion was impressive. And what helped here was a decline in credit loss expense from $65 million to $44.2 million. Obviously, the other factors that I mentioned were hopeful as well. But every little bit helps.

It is worth noting that non-interest income is also on the rise. For the final quarter of 2025, it was $132.2 million. That compared favorably to the $127.8 million generated a year prior. This increase helped some in pushing non-interest income up to $499.1 million for the year compared to the $459.1 million reported the previous year. A lot of this increase came from two categories. First and foremost, the company saw a nice jump in service charges on deposit accounts. But it also enjoyed an increase in trust and investment management fee revenue. These metrics grew from $106.2 million to $121.6 million and from $165.3 million to $177.1 million, respectively. At the end of the day, rising revenue translates to higher profits also. For the final quarter, the company generated net income of $164.6 million. And for the year as a whole, it amounted to $641.9 million. A year earlier, these metrics were $153.2 million and $575.9 million, respectively.

The market has certainly made it a point to reward the company for these strong results. In the chart above, you can see that the business is trading at a price-to-earnings multiple of 14.9. That's unfortunately well above the 10 maximum that I typically look for. And it also puts the company above four of the five banks that I compared it to in that chart. The chart below, meanwhile, looks at the picture through the lens of the price-to-book and price-to-tangible-book multiples. In both instances, four of the five institutions were cheaper than it is. And honestly, with both of these metrics trading at more than double book value, that is a big valuation red flag.

To some extent, a premium over the competition and on an absolute basis can be justified when asset quality is high. And certainly, that is the case here. In the first chart below, you can see that Cullen/Frost Bankers boasts a return on assets reading of 1.22%. That's comfortably higher than the 1% minimum that I look for. However, three of the five banks that I compared it to are superior to it on this basis. The picture does look better when it comes to return on equity. The 14.80% reading that we get is above the 10% minimum I look for. On top of this, it puts the company above four of the five competitors.

In addition to the company boasting impressive asset quality, certain credit quality metrics are also solid. Take, if you will, the percentage of loans that are classified as non-accrual. That number was only 0.32% in the most recent quarter. That is comfortably below the 0.50% maximum that I am comfortable with. Furthermore, only 0.13% of its assets are classified as non-accrual as well. Considering that I have a 0.50% threshold for that, the situation on that front is even more impressive.

Takeaway

If we are to talk about quality, I would say that Cullen/Frost Bankers certainly deserves attention. It is a high-quality institution that's growing where it matters most. Credit quality metrics are solid, and asset quality is impressive. Management is so confident, in fact, that during their third quarter earnings call, they announced a new $300 million share buyback program. But honestly, I can't get behind the stock as it's priced now. At the end of the day, I think that it justifies more of a ‘hold’ rating than it does anything else.

data.title: Nvidia Should Double-Beat Again (Earnings Preview)
data.description:
Consolidating Before Likely Beating

Nvidia Corporation (NVDA) stock has been consolidating since early August 2025, basically showing flat price action for the past six months:

The growth investors' focus has shifted dramatically over the past months – it's not what we saw in 2022-2024, when everyone was seeing NVDA as the main AI beneficiary on the market. A lot of that optimism from the past has already been priced in, and now more people have started to ask questions like, "What if the ROIC on AI CAPEX is low, and hyperscalers start to cancel their orders for NVDA's offerings shortly?" The premium in NVDA's valuation has evaporated as a result of such questions and the surrounding uncertainty that prevents us from definitively addressing that type of concern.

However, I don't think the current concerns about Nvidia's market standing are fully justified. In other words, we see that the demand side remains strong, and although the market is shaking under the weight of the new CAPEX wave, Nvidia shouldn't be negatively affected – at least not in the foreseeable future. The forward earnings are still being adjusted higher every time Nvidia reports fresh earnings, and for the level of growth that's going to come in the next couple of years, the stock is undervalued when judged under conventional metrics.

I think that the current consolidation is a healthy thing ahead of the upcoming earnings report, which, in my opinion, will only strengthen bullish conviction and lead to another round of revenue/EPS estimates revisions to the upside. If the stock price doesn't move in that case, it's going to make NVDA deeply undervalued on a forward basis, because it's already quite cheap. I'm leaving my "Buy" on NVDA with no changes ahead of the upcoming print (02/25/2026).

The Core Bullish Thesis On NVDA

Before jumping right to the consensus expectations and what management guided a few months ago, I'd like to discuss specific catalysts that I believe should lead to NVDA's continued strength ahead.

First, we're getting to the point when Nvidia should prove that its AI operating system – not just chips and racks, but also everything around it – can be efficiently monetized (i.e., at a sufficient margin) amid the rise in competition. The end market remains stable, at least it seems to be so, because we're moving towards inference from training, and the latter requires a lot of compute and reliable ecosystems, which is exactly what Nvidia has developed (i.e., Blackwell platform, Hopper GPUs H100/H200, and TensorRT-LLM software). The AI inference TAM should more than double in just five years.

What I liked in NVDA's approach is that they understand that their systems are currently arguably superior to everything else on the market, so they started to sell "automated miners" (Agentic Software), not just raw GPUs. For example, Nvidia AI Enterprise software now costs ~$4,500 per GPU per year – that's a high-margin recurring revenue stream that doesn't require constant hardware creation and hence limits the firm's consolidated COGS. It's changing the whole cyclicality story that Nvidia, like an ordinary semiconductor company, used to have in the previous industry cycles. Plus, they introduced "Digital Workers" powered by NIMs that give the firm a moat I don't think any other company can replicate or achieve any time soon. From that, I don't think NVDA's margins will be hit as hard as they could have been if there were no software layers.

What many people miss when it comes to Nvidia's medium-term prospects is the fact that they're basically controlling the whole industry's hardware pace. Why? We know that by late 2026, TSMC (TSM) is about to reach a capacity of 150,000 CoWoS wafers per month, and Nvidia will get ~60% of that output. That's called a "strategic monopsony", when a single customer de facto controls the market. NVDA's peers, like AMD (AMD), while I like its prospects as well, will be left to fight for the remaining "spillover" capacity in that case. As I see it, NVDA will be dictating the industry's development wave and cycle as it goes from the Blackwell ramp to its most ambitious project to date – the Vera Rubin (R100) architecture.

The Rubin platform is going to see the light by late 2026, and it integrates the Vera CPU (88 custom Olympus Arm cores), the Rubin GPU, NVLink 6 switches, the ConnectX-9 SuperNIC, the BlueField-4 DPU, and the Spectrum-6 Ethernet switch, according to NVDA's press release. This stack is important for NVDA's clientele because, in terms of the inference performance, it can be 5x more efficient, and it can also train different LLMs 3.5x better. It's also going to cut the cost per token for inference by up to 10x, needing only 1/4 of the number of GPUs that Blackwell needs these days. According to Introl's data, the new platform is currently in full production, so we might be only a few months away from another upgrade cycle that can boost Nvidia's earnings, as we saw with the Blackwell adoption last time.

Previewing Nvidia's Upcoming Earnings

Now, let's move to the earnings preview itself.

Despite the stock being flat over the past half year, we can see from Seeking Alpha's data that analysts have been busy revising the firm's forward EPS figures – in some cases, like in H1 2029, the consensus numbers have managed to double.

Nvidia reports for its fiscal Q4 2026 on February 25, 2026, and when the management had a post-earnings call last time, it guided for revenues of $65.00 billion (+/- 2%) with a non-GAAP gross margin at 75% (+/- 50 bps). Throughout fiscal 2027, the management said they were going to hold the GP margin at mid-70s, which definitely drove up the analysts' revisions for FY2027 that we see above.

Anyway, the current consensus (43 analysts) is looking for $65.58 billion in Q4 sales, which is below the high end of management's guidance. It's actually quite interesting because in the past few quarters, Nvidia has beaten its own high-end guidance with a comfortable margin of safety.

Recall the past few quarters. For Q1 2026, the guidance was at $43 billion plus/minus 2%, so the upper end was at $43.86 billion; the actual revenue came in at $44.06 billion, beating that upper end. For Q2 2026, the outlook for revenue was set at $45 billion (again, plus or minus 2%). The company beat the higher end by ~1.8%, showing $46.74 billion instead of $45.9 billion. For Q3, Nvidia projected $54 billion +/- 2%, and the actual Q3 revenue was $57 billion, heavily beating the upper end by over 3.4%.

Source: Nova Capital's article [Feb. 09, 2026]

I don't claim that past beats guarantee future ones, but I think you'll agree that the fact that today's revenue consensus is $720 million below the high-end guidance that had been consistently beaten before looks a bit suspicious. When there's this level of consistency, we usually see a premium on top of the high end. In my opinion, analysts have decided to form this consensus to play it safe ahead of earnings and let NVDA beat again (at least on the top line). It's just my theory; call it a gut feeling.

Assuming that we see a similar net income margin to the January quarter of FY 2024 (given the GP margin similarity), and assuming that Nvidia beats the high end of the guidance by 1% the way it did in the past few quarters, the firm's non-GAAP net income should amount to roughly $37.50 billion. Let's say that the number of shares outstanding in Q4 drops to 24.4 billion – in this case, the implied non-GAAP EPS arrives at $1.54, which is 2 cents above the current EPS consensus:

As a result, my calculations point to $66,963 in Q4 revenues and ~$1.53 in Q4 EPS – these are 2.1% and 1.11% above the current consensus, respectively. It doesn't look like much, but it's in sync with what we've seen so far in the past few years:

If my calculations make sense, then the analysts are likely to upgrade the firm's EPS and revenue figures for FY 2027-2029. And the stock is very likely to reprice higher on that analysts' action because the stock has already become quite cheap after the previous revisions.

I expect an eventual boost of 5% to the FY 2027 EPS consensus after the Q4 print and after the dust settles. At 25–30x earnings, that's giving NVDA a price target of ~$223.49/share, which is 22–23% above the current stock price. Wall Street analysts seem to have a higher PT in mind:

Where Can I Be Wrong?

I may underestimate the level of fear in the market regarding the lack of AI-driven ROIC – if Nvidia beats the upcoming earnings, but the management fails to convince the market participants about the sustainability of the demand side, we'll likely see a post-earnings slump that can last for longer than I expect.

And I can be wrong in my calculations of the Q4 revenues and EPS figures. Actually, I admit that I'm likely wrong on both of them because I only assumed the margins and share count for the quarter. It's normal for any financial model that forecasts anything. But my inputs may be off by a mile, and not in my favor. So, if NVDA fails to double-beat again, the stock price can drop meaningfully even if the management tries to reshift the audience's focus to the Rubing platform or anything else.

The Bottom Line

While I see some risks for Nvidia, and I understand the market's concern in general, I still think the stock has become quite cheap for the level of growth that the underlying business can achieve over the next years thanks to its unique market standing and new offerings. My calculations suggest that NVDA is likely to beat the upcoming earnings report, and if it does, the stock price is likely to react positively. My 12-month PT is 22-23% above the current stock price, so I remain bullish on NVDA in the medium term.

Thank you for reading!

data.title: Madrigal Pharmaceuticals: What To Expect With Q4 Earnings Coming Thursday
data.description:
Last year, I initiated Madrigal Pharmaceuticals, Inc. (MDGL) as a Strong Buy due to the blockbuster pace of commercialization for Rezdiffra following FDA approval in March 2024. Since then, the stock has appreciated from $293 to $465, a robust gain of 58% outpacing the S&P 500. However, this gain is even more impressive considering that the stock has recently pulled back by nearly 25% from its highs in December. The company's drug Rezdiffra is the primary investment thesis with a massive first-mover advantage in the Metabolic Dysfunction-Associated Steatohepatitis (MASH) market.

As of Q3 2025, Rezdiffra presented investors with rapid growth, achieving net sales of $287.3 million, with active patients on therapy numbering >29,500. Madrigal is scheduled to report Q4 earnings on Thursday, February 19th.

Investors are expecting continued growth, with analysts estimating revenues to reach $312.7M (an 8.8% QoQ growth rate from $287.3M in Q3). This explosive growth is anticipated to power Madrigal to positive earnings sometime this year; in fact, some analysts expect this quarter to achieve an inflection point to positive EPS.

The company now presents a market capitalization of $10.5B, arguably expensive at ~9x annualized Rezdiffra sales reported in Q3. However, analysts at Cantor Fitzgerald anticipate a continuing growth trend in MASH to reach ~$7B in peak sales. If this is correct, the stock is still cheap, and Madrigal has been proposed as a top buyout candidate that could be attractive as a 'bolt-on' acquisition, particularly for major Pharma replacing lost revenue from a patent cliff.

Given the size of the global market, Madrigal's continuing execution due to first-mover advantage in MASH, a pull-back in the stock price, and likely M&A activity, I reiterate my Strong Buy rating on Madrigal ahead of Q4 earnings.

Madrigal: Q4 2025 Earnings Preview

When Madrigal reports on Thursday, the headline number will be net sales, but investors should also watch for a bottom line inflection and sales guidance in 2026.

In Q3, GAAP earnings were obscured by business development costs (specifically licensing of an oral GLP-1), sending SG&A, R&D, and 'other' expenses up to $383M in Q3 from $251M in the prior quarter. This led to a loss of $5.08/share, and this quarter the consensus is for Madrigal to lose ~$0.52/share. However, a modest revenue beat and disciplined spending led Zacks Investment Research to expect positive EPS ($0.04) this quarter. This would be a major psychological turning point, de-risking the company financially.

In addition, investors will be focused on active patients on therapy, expecting robust growth to continue from the >29,500 patients in Q3. With the consensus seeing QoQ growth slowing to ~9%, this implies 32,000 patients. However, patient growth was ~28% QoQ in Q3. Should patient growth continue at nearly this rate, an upside surprise would be expected. Finally, Madrigal will provide 2026 sales guidance with the consensus modeling roughly $1.5B. If management's outlook exceeds that, a rally back to $500/share will be the likely outcome.

Madrigal Selected Data* Q4 Bull Case Q4 Consensus Q3 Actual 2026 Consensus
Revenue >$325M $312.7M $287.3M $1.46B
GAAP EPS Positive EPS ($0.54) ($5.08) $2.35
Active Patients >34,000 ~32,000 >29,500 --
Total Expenses <$295M ~$295M $383.1M --

*Data from Seeking Alpha.

Below are the numbers from Q3 2025. Last quarter, the number of active patients grew to >29,500 (28% QoQ), slowing slightly from the patient growth seen in Q2 (35.3%). Sales grew slightly faster, reaching $287.3M (35% QoQ), moderating from the growth rate seen in Q2 (55.0% QoQ).

While the growth trend is clearly slowing, the current expected rate of growth (8.8% QoQ) seems very attainable in light of the robust growth seen in the previous quarter. The consensus sees the growth trend slowing considerably, creating the possibility of an upside surprise.

The total addressable market (TAM) for MASH remains more than 10x larger in the US than the current patients under treatment. In total, management identified a potential market of 315,000 patients, but this still represents only 10% of the total MASH patients who have been diagnosed. The continuing adoption of Rezdiffra for MASH bodes well for a steadily increasing market for the drug.

In summary, the most important numbers from this week's earnings report are Rezdiffra sales in Q4 and guidance for 2026. However, an inflection to positive quarterly earnings may be possible as well.

Madrigal: Pipeline and Expansion Opportunities from J.P. Morgan 2026

In August 2025, the European Commission approved Rezdiffra, more than doubling the number of potential MASH patients to an estimated 370,000. This bodes well for continued growth in Q4 and 2026 as Rezdiffra only began selling in its first European market (Germany) in September 2025.

There are now two drugs approved for the treatment of MASH; the other is Novo Nordisk A/S's (NVO) GLP-1 drug Wegovy. Competition will be discussed more below; however, at the J.P. Morgan Healthcare conference, Madrigal made the point that MASH is similar to other large therapeutic categories, which grew substantially over time.

This is driven by the lack of treatment options before 2024, so MASH is underdiagnosed. The 29,500 patients on the drug as of Q3 represent less than 10% of diagnosed patients (315,000 in the US). However, 90% of MASH patients remain undiagnosed. In this manner, having new treatment options drives diagnosis, presenting investors with a long growth runway. Ultimately, management believes MASH can become competitive with IBD, RA, and psoriasis, which are all large markets with more than $20B in sales. These markets grew substantially after drugs became available to treat them.

Currently, Rezdiffra is FDA-approved for MASH patients with moderate to advanced fibrosis (Stages F2/F3). The primary pipeline opportunity for Madrigal is to expand the approval of Rezdiffra to Stage F4. F4 patients are the sickest, and it is a sizable population, estimated at 245,000 patients in the US. Madrigal has fully enrolled a Phase 3 trial with an estimated primary completion date in late 2026 or early 2027. If the expansion is approved, this lengthens the growth runway even more by nearly doubling the addressable patient population.

Finally, Madrigal is building its early-stage portfolio to defend its leadership. The company recently licensed an oral GLP-1 for combination studies with Rezdiffra in MASH. Madrigal also licensed an siRNA for similar combination studies. These silent genes are implicated in MASH; the combination with Rezdiffra may lead to improved patient outcomes. These are early-stage, thus not near commercialization yet.

Therefore, Madrigal has three major expansion catalysts in the coming year. First, geographic expansion to Europe began in Q4. A successful launch in Europe should continue growth at a robust rate. Second, with two drugs now approved for MASH, doctors are becoming more aware of its underdiagnosis. Finally, a Phase 3 study is underway in Stage F4 patients. If successful, Rezdiffra will become the only drug approved for MASH in Stage F4.

The Pullback: Why GLP-1 Fears Are Overblown

Since hitting all-time highs in December, Madrigal has retraced its gains by roughly 25%. While some of this pullback is natural profit-taking after a strong run, a primary bearish thesis has emerged that oral Wegovy, launched by Novo in January, will render Rezdiffra obsolete.

Wall Street loves to turn every competition into a zero-sum game; however, there are two problems with this thesis. The first is that ~70% of GLP-1 patients discontinue on semaglutide (the active ingredient of Wegovy) within 12 months. Thus, a patient on Rezdiffra can remain on the drug for longer. According to Consumer Edge, approximately 85% of patients taking Rezdiffra remain on therapy 4 months later, compared to ~75% for Wegovy.

Secondly, Madrigal has shown that Rezdiffra is complementary when dosed in combination with a GLP-1. This is why the company chose to in-license one. Thus, physicians are increasingly looking at these drugs as synergistic. Oral Wegovy treats weight, while Rezdiffra treats the fibrosis. Instead of stealing market share, oral Wegovy may actually increase the market size for Rezdiffra, as data show that physicians prefer using both drugs together.

The narrative that Madrigal is becoming obsolete due to the commercial rollout of oral Wegovy has offered investors an attractive pullback to buy shares at lower prices. If this narrative proves incorrect, as I believe it will, it also presents a 'wall of worry' for the stock to climb in the coming year.

Analyst Consensus Expectations and the Possible Fair Value Upside

Investors should expect volatility because there is substantial disagreement in the long term about what peak sales of Rezdiffra will be. Barclays recently set a $964/share price target for the stock, and some analysts see sales of Rezdiffra peaking above even the $7B forecast that was discussed above.

Piper Sandler also sees a path to $900, modeling peak sales that could exceed $7B. These bullish targets are driven by market expansion and success in the F4 Cirrhosis population. However, more conservative estimates target $550-$600, modeling greater impact from patients transitioning to GLP-1. As noted above, I believe these concerns are misplaced and side with the bulls.

However, due to the substantial operational leverage, higher prices seem likely. Consider that $2.94B (the consensus for 2028 sales) would be expected to generate $37.84 in EPS. Geographic expansion alone seems likely to deliver sales in this range, and if that happens, Madrigal trades for 12.3x 2028 EPS. The inflection to positive earnings this year should be a bullish catalyst for the stock.

Conclusion

In the coming year, the question for Madrigal will be how Rezdiffra sales hold up compared to the consensus expectation of $1.46B. With geographical expansion, increasing diagnosis, and a pipeline catalyst nearing the end of 2026, new all-time highs seem likely in the coming year.

My 12-month price target is $665 and targets 43% appreciation from the current market price. I believe the trajectory of sales will justify a peak projection of at least $5B, and once that happens, a 3x multiple or approximately 10x 2026 sales seems very reasonable.

Investors are being offered an attractive pull-back in Madrigal shares. Therefore, I reiterate my Strong Buy rating ahead of Q4 earnings.

data.title: British American Tobacco: Shifting My Conviction Lower (Downgrade)
data.description:
It's time to revisit British American Tobacco p.l.c. (BTI), which I last covered in August 2024, where I communicated a bullish view on the basis of renewed growth and solid balance sheet management. However, given the material passage of time, additional corporate strategy announcements, and British American Tobacco's recent earnings release, I've decided to re-engage; herewith are my latest thoughts.

Stock Performance Update

British American Tobacco's ADRs have delivered around 94.41% in total return since I first covered the stock in July 2022. In addition, 80.56% in gains have been delivered since I covered it in August 2024, most likely on the basis of popularity among dividend-seeking staple investors, new growth drivers, and equity market-wide support.

Let's move into a shorter-term view—the iShares Global Consumer Staples ETF (KXI) has delivered really strong year-to-date price returns, eclipsing British American Tobacco. However, carry has yet to be accounted for this year, which is important to note as British American Tobacco has a 5.39% trailing dividend yield, while KXI possesses a lower trailing yield of around 2.02%.

Starting January 1st, 2015, I formulated a z-score and calculated the 52-week rolling score to be 0.14, declining from a standard deviation of 2+, witnessed during stages in 2025. The z-score has drifted beyond levels of two sigma rich and two sigma cheap on numerous occasions since January 2015.

A z-score rolling 104 weeks shows that British American Tobacco ADR has shifted down to 0.91 sigma rich from a 2025 high of 3+ sigma rich.

Overall, we've seen British American Tobacco deliver relative underperformance versus other consumer defensive stocks. Idiosyncratic factors have clearly played a role, but a comparison to Altria Group (MO) shows a clear sector-specific lag—British American Tobacco has outperformed Altria, and both have lagged consumer staples over a five-year period.

Fundamental Review

Before I get into a review of the financials, I want to highlight British American Tobacco's re-aligned corporate strategy, whereby it's working toward “Building a Smokeless World.” In my view, cigarette sales were reliable for a prolonged period, but a shift in consumer trends to smokeless products presents uncertainty. Initial growth has been promising, but I am unsure about the sustainability of modern trends. In other words, I'm unsure how things will pan out; I used to view British American Tobacco as a staple, but I now view it as a thesis trade, which arguably changes the risk-return profile.

In another corporate strategy announcement, the company has committed to a cost-cutting strategy whereby it plans to implement optimisation via AI as part of its Fit2Win cost-cutting program, which aims to achieve £600 million by the end of 2028. While I am not in favour of people losing their jobs to technology, I think this will likely add productivity gains, balancing the uncertainty faced by a pivot away from cigarette sales and into smokeless products. Do I dismiss uncertainty? No, not at all, but I acknowledge the profit and loss buffer.

Let's revise the company's key operating figures and financials.

British American Tobacco recently revealed its prelim results for the calendar year-end of 2025, revealing the following:

  • Revenue grew by 2.1% in constant currency, down 1% on FX adjustments.
  • Cigarette sales volume is down 8% year-over-year.
  • Smokeless products grew to 18.2% of group revenue.
  • New categories' revenue growth reached 7% year-over-year.
  • Adjusted operating profits up by 2.3% to 44%.

A detailed view of the firm's P&L is available below.

A regional view shows that sales are still very much U.S.-based, but diversification across emerging markets is evident. I think emerging market expansion is a good move, due to their growth optics. However, factors such as threats from regional government instability, price advantages, and illicit trade need to be considered. These were some of the key reasons why British American Tobacco is withdrawing from South Africa.

A closer look at the growth engine, namely “new products,” is shown below, with Vapour products leading growth, coherently with the e-cigarettes and vapour market's forecasted annual growth rates of 30.6% (until 2030). I believe it's time for British American Tobacco to innovate and align with new trends, which it has evidently done. However, my argument is this: Vapour and smokeless products are trending, but the trend is new, and the sustainability thereof is unknown. I'm not arguing that British American Tobacco's growth won't sustain; I'm arguing that I dislike the uncertainty, because trend sustainability is difficult to determine. This is somewhat synonymous with the Cannabis industry, which, by the way, British American Tobacco also has a stake in through ownership of Organigram.

The company's balance sheet remains clean with a targeted net debt/EBITDA ratio of between 2.5 and 2x for 2026, which I deem quite low for a consumer staples company operating at operating margins above 40%. Leverage could be pushed up artificially by buybacks. British American Tobacco plans on buying back £1.3 billion in shares in 2026, but it's an implicit guarantee, not a commitment that would strain the balance sheet. I'd also highlight that cash and equivalents ended at ~£3.8 billion for 2025. That said, I'd keep a close eye on the elevated intangible assets load of near ~£87 billion, which might add subjectivity to valuations.

The outlook for British American Tobacco consists of additional decline in global cigarette volume, reiterating the pivot into new consumer trends. Concurrently, the broad-based growth outlook is solid (3%-5% in revenue and 5%-8% in EPS), driven by the secular growth in new products and markups in older products. However, as reiterated on numerous occasions in this piece, my reason for skepticism isn't the viability of new products; it's the business' pivot out of a long-run ballast and into new trends, which arguably turns it into a thesis trade, as opposed to a reliable, low-volatility, high-dividend machine—I have numerous thesis trades in my portfolio that rely on more robust markets (technology being one).

Systematic Market Commentary

Fidelity recently updated its business cycle map for Q1 2026. It holds the view that the US and numerous EMs are both in late cycle, and the Eurozone and China provide some insulation. The view is merely a mapping of the economy and not a certainty. That said, it aligns with my take on the business cycle, which is that the economy is shifting down into late-cycle growth (explained in numerous of my recent articles on Seeking Alpha). It is no coincidence that staple stocks have rallied in conjunction with the shifting business cycle, which is usually the case during late-cycle environments.

I calculated z-scores for KXI versus the iShares Global Consumer Discretionary ETF (RXI). It's shown on a 52-week rolling basis that the staples vehicle is now 1 sigma rich versus the discretionary vehicle after telling year-to-date outperformance. This aligns with my late-cycle view.
